content_markdown: The power emitted as electromagnetic radiation by the Sun is approximately $4 \times 10^{26} \text{ W}$. The radius of the orbit of Mars around the Sun is approximately $2 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$. What is the best estimate for the power incident on an area of $1 \text{ m}^2$ at the radius of Mars' orbit?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: To find the power incident on $1 \text{ m}^2$ (the intensity), we use the inverse square law: $I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}$. Substituting the values: $I = \frac{4 \times 10^{26}}{4 \times \pi \times (2 \times 10^{11})^2}$. This simplifies to $\frac{10^{26}}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{22}} = \frac{10^4}{4\pi}$. Since $4\pi \approx 12.5$, the value is roughly $10^4 / 12.5$, which is closest to $10^3 \text{ W}$.
